fr:title.text: negation invariance of the square root relation

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: negation invariance of the square root relation

fr:title.text: negation invariance of the square root relation › forest://jonmsterling/jms-00IO/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: negation invariance of the square root relation › jms-00IO/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By relational extensionality, it suffices to check that for any x\in \mathbb {R}_{\geq 0} and y\in \mathbb {R}, we have x\mathrel {{\mathopen {}\left (\mathsf {neg}\circ \mathsf {sq}\right )\mathclose {}}} y if and only if x\mathrel {\mathsf {sq}}y. We compute:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} x\mathrel {{\mathopen {}\left (\mathsf {neg}\circ \mathsf {sq}\right )\mathclose {}}} y &\Longleftrightarrow \exists z\in \mathbb {R}\mathpunct {.} x\mathrel {\mathsf {sq}}z \land z \mathrel {\mathsf {neg}}y \\ &\Longleftrightarrow \exists z\in \mathbb {R}\mathpunct {.} x=z^2 \land z =-y \\ &\Longleftrightarrow x={\mathopen {}\left (-y\right )\mathclose {}}^2 \\ &\Longleftrightarrow x = y^2 \\ &\Longleftrightarrow x\mathrel {\mathsf {sq}}y \end {aligned}

fr:title.text: negation invariance of the square root relation

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: negation invariance of the square root relation

fr:title.text: negation invariance of the square root relation › forest://jonmsterling/jms-00IO/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: negation invariance of the square root relation › jms-00IO/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By relational extensionality, it suffices to check that for any x\in \mathbb {R}_{\geq 0} and y\in \mathbb {R}, we have x\mathrel {{\mathopen {}\left (\mathsf {neg}\circ \mathsf {sq}\right )\mathclose {}}} y if and only if x\mathrel {\mathsf {sq}}y. We compute:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} x\mathrel {{\mathopen {}\left (\mathsf {neg}\circ \mathsf {sq}\right )\mathclose {}}} y &\Longleftrightarrow \exists z\in \mathbb {R}\mathpunct {.} x\mathrel {\mathsf {sq}}z \land z \mathrel {\mathsf {neg}}y \\ &\Longleftrightarrow \exists z\in \mathbb {R}\mathpunct {.} x=z^2 \land z =-y \\ &\Longleftrightarrow x={\mathopen {}\left (-y\right )\mathclose {}}^2 \\ &\Longleftrightarrow x = y^2 \\ &\Longleftrightarrow x\mathrel {\mathsf {sq}}y \end {aligned}

fr:title.text: associativity and unit laws of relational composition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: associativity and unit laws of relational composition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Relational composition is associative and has the identity relation as a neutral element.

fr:title.text: associativity and unit laws of relational composition › forest://jonmsterling/jms-00IP/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: associativity and unit laws of relational composition › jms-00IP/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Using the above, we can compute the full composites:

For the right and left neutrality, we must prove that R\circ \mathsf {id}_{A} = R = \mathsf {id}_{B}\circ R for all r\colon A\mathbin {\nrightarrow } B. We prove only the first law, as the other proof is analogous:

A (binary) relation R from a set A to a set B, written R\colon A\mathbin {\nrightarrow } B or R\in \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left (A,B\right )\mathclose {}} is defined to be a subset R\subseteq A\times B. We shall typically write a\mathrel {R}b for {\mathopen {}\left (a,b\right )\mathclose {}}\in R. More generally, a relation between multiple sets {\mathopen {}\left (A_i\right )\mathclose {}}_{i\in I} is defined to be a subset of the cartesian product \prod _{i\in I} A_i.

In the simplest terms, a specification of a program is a relation that describes the possible input/output pairs that can occur. For example, the specification that a given program compute the square root is captured by the relation \mathsf {sq} \colon \mathbb {R}_{\geq 0} \mathbin {\nrightarrow } \mathbb {R} given by pairs {\mathopen {}\left (x,y\right )\mathclose {}} such that x = y^2.
